message.content:
You are an independent, adversarial fix-confirmation reviewer (reviewer B) for the hc-assay hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-9, latest commit). Confirm the fix is BOTH appropriate (correct root cause, sound approach, right layer) AND complete (no residual gap; regression test DISCRIMINATES — fails if reverted). Be skeptical; find holes. For a constrained finding (CI-1) judge the response given the real constraint.

FINDING P9-MO-2:
bare int()/float() parsing of env vars in seam.py (8 sites) and checkpoint.py (4 sites) raised an opaque "invalid literal" naming no var. Fix: new shared src/assay_engine/_envparse.py (int_env/float_env, empty treated as default, ValueError names the var), wired into seam.py + checkpoint.py. VERIFY: a malformed ASSAY_* numeric env raises a ValueError naming the var; seam.py/checkpoint.py no longer have bare int(/float( of os.environ/_env; existing behavior (defaults, monkeypatched _MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS) preserved. Tests: test_numeric_env_parsing_names_the_bad_var (+ existing persistence/observability int_env tests still pass).

Read the changed files and run what you can (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -m pytest -q <path>; "python -m pytest" not bare pytest; gh available for CI-1). Judge ONLY P9-MO-2. CONFIRMED only if appropriate AND complete; CONCERN if a real residual gap; REJECTED if wrong/non-discriminating. List concrete concerns (empty if none).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll review finding P9-MO-2. Let me start by examining the changed files.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git log --oneline -5 && echo "---" && git show --stat HEAD

content.content:
8c5d7e5 fix(hardening-p9): 5 confirmed incl. security regression of pass-8 unix-socket fix
6e3d6b4 Merge pull request #169 from [REDACTED]/harden/pass-8
71ed4d5 fix(hardening-p8): CI follow-ons surfaced by actually running CI (K-CI-3 + lockfile via-comments)
23de0f6 docs(hardening): record pass 8 — 8 confirmed incl. 3 CI-red blockers; main CI was failing
9998558 fix(hardening-p8): strengthen coverage-floor guard (K-OPS-3 confirm-concern)
---
commit 8c5d7e5268db467a3918f84ebcb9573cfef3334f
Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Wed Jun 17 12:29:53 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p9): 5 confirmed incl. security regression of pass-8 unix-socket fix
    
    All 2-agent verified TRUE_POSITIVE.
    
    - K-SEC-9-1 (high, SECURITY REGRESSION of pass-8 K-SEC-1): the Unix-socket allowance
      checked the whole host string, so libpq's comma multi-host 'host=/tmp,evil.com' was
      ACCEPTED (libpq tries the socket then falls back to the off-box TCP host → exfiltration
      past ADR-0003). Fix: is_local_socket_path rejects comma values; the DSN+query branches
      now SPLIT on ',' and validate every element (mirroring the authority branch). Verified:
      all multi-host bypasses rejected, legit single socket/loopback accepted.
    - CI-1 (high): branch protection is unavailable on this plan (private/free → 403), so the
      all-checks gate is unenforced — the mechanism behind the campaign's silently-red CI.
      Enabling it is out of scope (public repo would publish transcripts; plan upgrade is a
      billing decision). Compensating control: scripts/require_green_ci.sh (pre-merge gate),
      docs/MERGE-POLICY.md, and an honest all-checks caveat comment.
    - P9-PII-1 (med): operator machine/namespace hostname ([REDACTED][.local], [REDACTED])
      leaked into committed transcripts — no scrub rule covered it. Fix: _hostname_patterns
      derives the machine hostname (socket/platform/scutil) → redacts bare + .local; an
      ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS namespace host has only its .local form redacted (bare project token
      preserved; unrelated *.local untouched). Re-scrubbed corpus (grep now 0).
    - P9-MO-1 (med): confirm primitives raised an opaque TypeError when alpha was locked on
      neither hypothesis nor call; now a ValueError naming alpha and how to provide it.
    - P9-MO-2 (low): bare int()/float() env parsing in seam.py/checkpoint.py raised opaque
      errors naming no var. Fix: shared assay_engine._envparse (int_env/float_env) naming the
      var, wired into seam.py + checkpoint.py.
    
    Regression tests for every finding. Gates: ruff + format + mypy --strict clean; 563
    passed, 5 skipped; coverage floor enforced.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

 docs/MERGE-POLICY.md                               |  43 +++

 .../wf_f1d1aa22-666/agent-aded3f6e26e918f81.jsonl  |  84 ++++++
 .../agent-aded3f6e26e918f81.meta.json              |   1 +
 .../workflows/wf_f1d1aa22-666/journal.jsonl        |  32 +++
 .../tool-results/bl4aowxms.txt                     | 105 +++++++
 .../tool-results/blcaaq3iq.txt                     |  14 +
 .../tool-results/blwbmaupp.txt                     |  20 ++
 .../workflows/wf_7a766754-e04.json                 |   1 +
 .../workflows/wf_f1d1aa22-666.json                 |   1 +
 76 files changed, 2098 insertions(+), 49 deletions(-)

1	"""Parse numeric env vars with an error that NAMES the offending var (#H-022/#CV-S-1/#P9-MO-2).
2	
3	A bare ``int(os.environ[...])`` / ``float(...)`` raises an opaque "invalid literal for int()" that
4	names neither the variable nor where it fired (usually at import, before any handler). These
5	helpers fall back to ``default`` (treating an empty value as unset) and, on a malformed value,
6	raise a ``ValueError`` naming the variable — the single source replacing the per-module copies that
7	had grown in ``observability/tracing.py`` and ``persistence/vectorstore.py``.
8	"""
9	
10	from __future__ import annotations
11	
12	import os
13	
14	
15	def _raw(name: str, default: str) -> str:
16	    val = os.environ.get(name)
17	    return val if val is not None and val != "" else default
18	
19	
20	def int_env(name: str, default: str) -> int:
21	    raw = _raw(name, default)
22	    try:
23	        return int(raw)
24	    except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc:
25	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}") from exc
26	
27	
28	def float_env(name: str, default: str) -> float:
29	    raw = _raw(name, default)
30	    try:
31	        return float(raw)
32	    except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc:
33	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be a number; got {raw!r}") from exc
34

input.description: Show diff for seam.py and checkpoint.py

content.content:
commit 8c5d7e5268db467a3918f84ebcb9573cfef3334f
Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Wed Jun 17 12:29:53 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p9): 5 confirmed incl. security regression of pass-8 unix-socket fix
    
    All 2-agent verified TRUE_POSITIVE.
    
    - K-SEC-9-1 (high, SECURITY REGRESSION of pass-8 K-SEC-1): the Unix-socket allowance
      checked the whole host string, so libpq's comma multi-host 'host=/tmp,evil.com' was
      ACCEPTED (libpq tries the socket then falls back to the off-box TCP host → exfiltration
      past ADR-0003). Fix: is_local_socket_path rejects comma values; the DSN+query branches
      now SPLIT on ',' and validate every element (mirroring the authority branch). Verified:
      all multi-host bypasses rejected, legit single socket/loopback accepted.
    - CI-1 (high): branch protection is unavailable on this plan (private/free → 403), so the
      all-checks gate is unenforced — the mechanism behind the campaign's silently-red CI.
      Enabling it is out of scope (public repo would publish transcripts; plan upgrade is a
      billing decision). Compensating control: scripts/require_green_ci.sh (pre-merge gate),
      docs/MERGE-POLICY.md, and an honest all-checks caveat comment.
    - P9-PII-1 (med): operator machine/namespace hostname ([REDACTED][.local], [REDACTED])
      leaked into committed transcripts — no scrub rule covered it. Fix: _hostname_patterns
      derives the machine hostname (socket/platform/scutil) → redacts bare + .local; an
      ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS namespace host has only its .local form redacted (bare project token
      preserved; unrelated *.local untouched). Re-scrubbed corpus (grep now 0).
    - P9-MO-1 (med): confirm primitives raised an opaque TypeError when alpha was locked on
      neither hypothesis nor call; now a ValueError naming alpha and how to provide it.
    - P9-MO-2 (low): bare int()/float() env parsing in seam.py/checkpoint.py raised opaque
      errors naming no var. Fix: shared assay_engine._envparse (int_env/float_env) naming the
      var, wired into seam.py + checkpoint.py.
    
    Regression tests for every finding. Gates: ruff + format + mypy --strict clean; 563
    passed, 5 skipped; coverage floor enforced.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

diff --git a/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py b/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py
index d779b73..53e4d01 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py
@@ -43,6 +43,7 @@ import threading
 from typing import Any, cast
 from urllib.parse import urlsplit
 
+from assay_engine._envparse import int_env
 from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri
 
 _DEFAULT_PG = "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay"
@@ -58,7 +59,7 @@ _MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY = 0x4153_5359
 # the bound (block indefinitely, the pre-#129 behavior). Read fresh on each bootstrap (#F-033) so
 # an env var set after import still takes effect; the module constant is the default + the value
 # tests monkeypatch.
-_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS = max(0, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS", "30000")))
+_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS = max(0, int_env("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS", "30000"))
 
 
 def _migration_lock_timeout_ms() -> int:
@@ -68,9 +69,9 @@ def _migration_lock_timeout_ms() -> int:
     monkeypatches ``_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS`` still drives the value.
     """
     raw = os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS")
-    if raw is None:
+    if raw is None or raw == "":
         return _MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS
-    return max(0, int(raw))
+    return max(0, int_env("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS", "30000"))  # names the var on error
 
 
 # Brief meta-lock guarding the process-local registries below (atexit registration, the open-pool
@@ -268,9 +269,9 @@ def get_checkpointer(use_memory: bool = False) -> Any:
             # threads, so a fixed max_size=8 can be saturated under concurrent reasoning+checkpoint
             # load on larger hardware. connect_timeout bounds an individual TCP connect so a
             # black-holed host fails fast instead of hanging bootstrap for the OS default.
-            min_size = max(0, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_POOL_MIN_SIZE", "1")))
-            max_size = max(min_size or 1, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_POOL_MAX_SIZE", "8")))
-            connect_timeout = max(1, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_POOL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT", "10")))
+            min_size = max(0, int_env("ASSAY_POOL_MIN_SIZE", "1"))
+            max_size = max(min_size or 1, int_env("ASSAY_POOL_MAX_SIZE", "8"))
+            connect_timeout = max(1, int_env("ASSAY_POOL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT", "10"))
             pool = ConnectionPool(
                 conn_str,
                 min_size=min_size,
diff --git a/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py b/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py
index ae8e431..24ada8c 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py
@@ -40,6 +40,7 @@ from enum import Enum
 from pathlib import Path
 from typing import Any, Callable, Iterator, Mapping, Protocol, cast
 
+from assay_engine._envparse import float_env, int_env
 from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
 from assay_engine._local import require_loopback_url
 
@@ -91,23 +92,23 @@ BULK_MODEL = _env("ASSAY_BULK_MODEL", "llama3.1:8b")
 BULK_BASE_URL = _env("ASSAY_BULK_BASE_URL", "http://localhost:11434")
 HIGH_STAKES_MODEL = os.environ.get("ASSAY_HIGH_STAKES_MODEL") or None
 
-BULK_TIMEOUT = max(1.0, float(_env("ASSAY_BULK_TIMEOUT", "120")))
+BULK_TIMEOUT = max(1.0, float_env("ASSAY_BULK_TIMEOUT", "120"))
 # Cap BULK generation length. ChatOllama/Ollama default to unbounded (-1); without a cap a
 # local model can run away to context exhaustion, and the wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a
 # call already blocked in the backend (a leaked pool slot). Bounding output tokens is the real
 # guard (LangChain's own ChatOllama example sets num_predict explicitly).
-BULK_NUM_PREDICT = max(1, int(_env("ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT", "2048")))
-HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT = max(1.0, float(_env("ASSAY_HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT", "300")))
+BULK_NUM_PREDICT = max(1, int_env("ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT", "2048"))
+HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT = max(1.0, float_env("ASSAY_HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT", "300"))
 
-MAX_RETRIES = max(0, int(_env("ASSAY_REASONING_RETRIES", "2")))
-BACKOFF_BASE = max(0.0, float(_env("ASSAY_REASONING_BACKOFF", "1.5")))
-RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF = max(0.0, float(_env("ASSAY_RATELIMIT_BACKOFF", "30")))
-RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES = max(0, int(_env("ASSAY_RATELIMIT_RETRIES", "5")))
+MAX_RETRIES = max(0, int_env("ASSAY_REASONING_RETRIES", "2"))
+BACKOFF_BASE = max(0.0, float_env("ASSAY_REASONING_BACKOFF", "1.5"))
+RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF = max(0.0, float_env("ASSAY_RATELIMIT_BACKOFF", "30"))
+RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES = max(0, int_env("ASSAY_RATELIMIT_RETRIES", "5"))
 # run_json re-rolls on a PARSE failure. This is independent of the transient/rate-limit retry
 # budget above (which applies per generation) so the two do not multiply into a retry storm; an
 # overall wall-clock deadline bounds the total regardless (#102).
-JSON_REROLLS = max(0, int(_env("ASSAY_JSON_REROLLS", "2")))
-RUN_JSON_DEADLINE = max(1.0, float(_env("ASSAY_RUN_JSON_DEADLINE", "600")))
+JSON_REROLLS = max(0, int_env("ASSAY_JSON_REROLLS", "2"))
+RUN_JSON_DEADLINE = max(1.0, float_env("ASSAY_RUN_JSON_DEADLINE", "600"))
 
 # OAuth subscription token name (external CLI contract — not renamed).
 OAUTH_TOKEN_ENV = "CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN"

content.text: Now let me look at the `_env` function in seam.py to understand the layering, and verify no bare int(/float( remain.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -nE 'int\(|float\(' src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py; echo "=== _env def in seam ==="; grep -n "_env\b\|def _env" src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py | head

input.description: Show vectorstore _int_env body and seam env reads

content.content:
def _int_env(name: str, default: str) -> int:
    """Parse an int env var with a clear error naming the var (#H-022).

    A bare ``int(os.environ[...])`` at import time raises an opaque ``ValueError: invalid literal
    for int()`` that doesn't say WHICH var was misconfigured, and it fires during import.
    """
    raw = os.environ.get(name, default)
    try:
        return int(raw)
    except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc:
        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}") from exc

content.text:
All remaining `os.environ` reads in seam.py are string reads (`_env`, `_truthy`, model name, OAuth token, env-dict passthrough) — no numeric parsing. The seam.py + checkpoint.py scope is fully clean.

One subtle behavioral note on the pre-existing private copies: `vectorstore._int_env` uses `os.environ.get(name, default)` which does NOT treat empty-string as default (an empty `ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT=""` would hit the int() and raise), whereas the new shared `int_env` treats empty as default. So they are not even behaviorally identical — another reason the docstring's "single source" claim is misleading. But again, this is orthogonal to P9-MO-2's actual scope.

Let me do a final confirmation that the full declared test set passes.

All tests pass. My assessment of P9-MO-2:

**Appropriate** — Root cause (bare `int()`/`float()` of env vars raising opaque "invalid literal" naming no var) is correctly addressed. The shared `_envparse.py` helper sits at the right layer; `ValueError` names the var, empty treated as default, original `(ValueError, TypeError)` semantics preserved. The `max(...)` clamps remain outside the parse, so negative/floor behavior is unchanged. The three remaining `int(`/`float(` in seam.py (lines 520/522/673) parse caller param dicts, not env vars, and already have their own typed-error contract — correctly out of scope.

**Complete** — Both declared files are fully migrated; no bare numeric env parse remains in either. The `_migration_lock_timeout_ms` empty-string fix is a genuine improvement (empty no longer crashes). The regression test discriminates: I verified the static-assertion regex matches all three pre-fix forms (so reverting any one fails the test) and ignores the out-of-scope param parses; the runtime assertion names the var. Monkeypatch and default behaviors verified preserved. Full env-parse test set green.

**Concern (minor, non-blocking, outside P9-MO-2's functional scope):** The new `_envparse.py` module docstring claims it is "the single source replacing the per-module copies that had grown in `observability/tracing.py` and `persistence/vectorstore.py`." That is false — both modules still define and use their own private `_int_env`, and `vectorstore._int_env` is not even behaviorally identical (it uses `os.environ.get(name, default)`, so an empty-string value is NOT treated as the default, unlike the new shared helper). This is a documentation overclaim, not a defect in the seam.py/checkpoint.py fix the finding is scoped to. The fix as scoped is correct and complete; the docstring should either be corrected or those two modules migrated to the shared helper.

input.rationale: P9-MO-2 is appropriate and complete within its declared scope (seam.py + checkpoint.py). Root cause correctly fixed: bare int()/float() of env vars raised an opaque 'invalid literal' naming no var; the new shared assay_engine._envparse (int_env/float_env) raises a ValueError naming the var, treats empty as default, and preserves (ValueError, TypeError) semantics. The max(...) clamps stay outside the parse so floor behavior is unchanged. Both files are fully migrated — grep shows no bare int(/float( wrapping os.environ or _env( remains; the three residual int(/float( in seam.py (lines 520/522/673) parse caller param dicts behind their own PermanentReasoningError contract and are correctly out of scope. All other os.environ reads in seam.py are string reads. Behavior verified at runtime: defaults, empty-string fallback, valid parse, var-named error, and the monkeypatched _MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS path (including the new empty-string fall-through) all hold. The regression test DISCRIMINATES: I confirmed its static-assertion regex matches all three pre-fix forms (so reverting any one fails) while ignoring the out-of-scope param parses, and its runtime assertion checks the var name. test_numeric_env_parsing_names_the_bad_var plus the existing persistence/observability int_env tests all pass.
concerns: Minor / outside P9-MO-2 functional scope: the new _envparse.py module docstring overclaims it is 'the single source replacing the per-module copies that had grown in observability/tracing.py and persistence/vectorstore.py' — but both modules still define and use their own private _int_env (tracing.py:32, vectorstore.py:20). They were NOT migrated. Moreover vectorstore._int_env uses os.environ.get(name, default) so it does NOT treat an empty-string value as the default, unlike the shared int_env — so the copies are not even behaviorally identical. Either correct the docstring or migrate those two modules. This is a documentation accuracy issue, not a defect in the seam.py/checkpoint.py fix.

content.text: P9-MO-2 reviewed: CONFIRMED. The fix is appropriate and complete within its declared scope (seam.py + checkpoint.py), with a discriminating regression test. I noted one minor non-blocking concern about a docstring overclaim in `_envparse.py` regarding tracing.py/vectorstore.py, which is outside this finding's functional scope.
